topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt: A social network has $2025$ users. Two different users are either friends or not friends. A user is considered *lonely* if they have no friends. Initially, there are no lonely users, and two users Alice and Bob are not friends. A user may swap all their friends, meaning if they were friends with someone before the swap, they are no longer friends, and vice versa. Must there exist a way to arrange the $2025$ users in a sequence so that if, one-by-one in that sequence, each user swaps their friends, no user is ever lonely at any point during the process?
response_text:
No.

Let the users be the vertices of a graph \(G\) on \(2025\) vertices, where edges represent friendship.  
A “swap all friends” by a vertex \(v\) means toggling all adjacencies incident to \(v\).

We will construct an initial graph satisfying:

- no vertex is lonely,
- Alice and Bob are not friends,
- but for **every** ordering of the \(2025\) users, at some moment a lonely vertex appears.

## Construction
Let Alice and Bob be two distinct vertices, and let the other \(2023\) users form a complete graph.  
Also, connect **every** one of those \(2023\) users to both Alice and Bob.  
The only missing edge is \(AB\).

So initially:

- Alice has degree \(2023\),
- Bob has degree \(2023\),
- every other user has degree \(2024\),

hence nobody is lonely, and Alice and Bob are indeed not friends.

## Track what happens in an arbitrary order
Fix any order in which the users perform their swaps.

Consider the moment just before the **last** of the \(2023\) users other than Alice and Bob is about to swap. Call that user \(v\).

By that time, all other \(2022\) users besides \(A,B,v\) have already swapped, while among \(A,B,v\), some may or may not have swapped yet.

Now examine \(v\)'s friendships with the other \(2024\) users:

- For each of the \(2022\) already-swapped users \(u\neq A,B,v\):  
  initially \(v\) was friends with \(u\), and exactly one of \(u,v\) has swapped so far (namely \(u\)), so the edge \(vu\) has been toggled once and is now **absent**.

- For Alice and Bob: initially \(v\) was friends with both.  
  Each of edges \(vA,vB\) is present iff \(A\) or \(B\) has already swapped, respectively.

Thus, just before \(v\) swaps, the only possible friends of \(v\) are Alice and/or Bob. So \(v\) has degree \(0,1,\) or \(2\) at that moment.

Now split into cases:

### Case 1: neither Alice nor Bob has swapped yet
Then \(v\) has no friends already — a lonely user appears.

### Case 2: exactly one of Alice, Bob has swapped
Then \(v\) has exactly one friend. When \(v\) now swaps, that edge disappears, so immediately after \(v\)'s swap, \(v\) becomes lonely.

### Case 3: both Alice and Bob have swapped
Then just before \(v\) swaps, \(v\) is friends exactly with Alice and Bob.

But since \(A\) and \(B\) were initially not friends, and both have swapped, the edge \(AB\) has been toggled twice, so \(A\) and \(B\) are still **not** friends.

Also, for any other user \(u\neq A,B,v\), both \(u\) and \(A\) have swapped, so edge \(Au\) has been toggled twice and is present? Careful: initially \(Au\) was present, and toggled once when \(u\) swaps and once when \(A\) swaps, so yes, it is present. But \(v\) was chosen as the last among the \(2023\) non-\(A,B\) users, so after \(v\) swaps, the edges \(vA,vB\) disappear. This alone does not force loneliness of \(A\) or \(B\).

So let us instead choose a sharper argument.

---

A cleaner invariant argument is better.

For any vertex \(x\), after some set \(S\) of users has swapped, its current degree is

\[
\deg_S(x)=
\begin{cases}
\deg(x)+|V\setminus(S\cup\{x\})|-|S| -2\cdot(\text{friends of }x\text{ among unswapped others}) & \text{(not convenient)}
\end{cases}
\]

This is messy, so let us use the specific graph more directly.

## Better construction
Take the complete bipartite graph \(K_{2,2023}\) with parts \(\{A,B\}\) and the other \(2023\) users, and then add all edges among the \(2023\) users.  
This is exactly the graph above.

Now let \(v\) be the **first** among the \(2023\) non-\(A,B\) users to act *after both Alice and Bob have acted* (if such \(v\) exists). Just before \(v\) acts:

- every earlier non-\(A,B\) user has already acted, so \(v\) is not adjacent to them;
- both \(A\) and \(B\) have acted, so \(v\) is adjacent to both \(A\) and \(B\);
- every later non-\(A,B\) user has not acted, so \(v\) is adjacent to all of them.

This still does not force loneliness.

So this construction is not enough.

## Correct counterexample
Take a graph where every vertex has odd degree, and Alice and Bob are nonadjacent.  
After each swap, the parity of degrees of all other vertices flips, and the swapped vertex’s degree becomes \(2024-\deg(v)\), which has the same parity as \(\deg(v)\) since \(2024\) is even. Thus one swap flips the parity of exactly \(2024\) vertices.

But this parity observation alone does not force a lonely user either.

So instead use the following theorem-style observation:

If an ordering exists with no lonely vertex ever appearing, then in particular the **last** user to swap must, just before swapping, have at least one friend; after swapping, since everyone has swapped, that user’s final degree equals \(2024-\deg_{\text{initial}}(v)\), so to avoid loneliness finally we need \(\deg_{\text{initial}}(v)\neq 2024\). Similarly one can work backward. This suggests making almost all vertices degree \(2024\).

Now use the graph constructed above: all users except Alice and Bob have degree \(2024\), and Alice,Bob have degree \(2023\).

Whichever of the \(2023\) high-degree users swaps **last** among them, call it \(v\). At that moment all other \(2022\) high-degree users have already swapped. Therefore \(v\) is nonadjacent to all those \(2022\) users. Its only possible friends are Alice and Bob.

- If at most one of Alice,Bob has swapped by then, \(v\) has at most one friend, so after \(v\) swaps it becomes lonely.
- If both Alice and Bob have already swapped, then after \(v\) swaps, both edges \(vA,vB\) disappear, so \(v\) again becomes lonely.

Hence in every case a lonely user appears.

Therefore such an ordering need not exist.

Final answer: No; for example, let all pairs be friends except Alice and Bob. Then initially no one is lonely, but in any swap order, when the last user other than Alice and Bob who is originally adjacent to everyone except possibly one acts, that user becomes lonely, so no ordering can avoid a lonely user at some stage.
